title: What to know about Notopterygium incisium  (qianghuo) before using it medicinally
section_medicine_ailments: Notopterygium incisium helps healing several common ailments, such as arthritis, rheumatism, and gout. This traditional Chinese medicine herb has been used for centuries to treat various inflammatory conditions that cause pain, stiffness, and swelling in the joints. Its anti-inflammatory properties help reduce joint inflammation and alleviate symptoms of arthritis, allowing patients to move with greater ease and flexibility. Additionally, Notopterygium incisium has also been shown to be effective in treating gout, a condition characterized by sudden and severe attacks of joint pain, caused by high levels of uric acid in the blood. By reducing inflammation and promoting the elimination of excess uric acid from the body, this herb helps alleviate symptoms of gout, such as intense pain, swelling, and redness of the affected joints. Furthermore, Notopterygium incisium has also been used to treat other inflammatory conditions, including sprains, strains, and tendonitis, making it a valuable natural remedy for promoting joint health and overall well-being.
section_medicine_properties: Notopterygium incisium has several medicinal properties, such as anti-inflammatory, antipyretic, and analgesic effects. The plant's active compounds, including sesquiterpenes and flavonoids, have been shown to exhibit significant inhibitory activities against pro-inflammatory enzymes and cytokines, making it a valuable natural remedy for the treatment of various inflammatory diseases, including arthritis, gout, and musculoskeletal disorders. Additionally, Notopterygium incisium has been traditionally used in Chinese medicine to reduce fever and relieve pain, likely due to its ability to suppress the production of prostaglandins and interleukin-1β. The plant's analgesic properties have also been attributed to the presence of volatile oils, which can effectively alleviate headaches, neuralgia, and other types of pain. Furthermore, studies have demonstrated that Notopterygium incisium exhibits antimicrobial and antifungal activities, making it a potential natural remedy for wound infections and skin conditions.
section_medicine_parts: The most commonly used parts of Notopterygium incisium for medicinal purposes are the rhizome, stems, and leaves. The rhizome is considered to be the most valuable part of the plant, as it contains a high concentration of active compounds such as sesquiterpenoids, phenolic acids, and volatile oils. These compounds have been shown to exhibit anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and antipyretic properties, making Notopterygium incisium a popular natural remedy for the treatment of various pain-related conditions, including headaches, rheumatism, and arthritis. The stems and leaves of the plant are also used medicinally, often in combination with the rhizome, as they contain smaller amounts of active compounds that complement its therapeutic effects. For example, the stems have been shown to possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, while the leaves have been traditionally used to treat respiratory issues such as bronchitis and asthma due to their expectorant and anti-inflammatory properties.
section_medicine_side_effects: When used improperly, Notopterygium incisium increases the chances of experiencing side effects, such as gastrointestinal disturbances, including nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. Additionally, improper use can lead to allergic reactions, which may manifest as skin rashes, itching, or hives in some individuals. Furthermore, Notopterygium incisium can interact with other medications, potentially leading to adverse effects when combined with blood thinners, diabetes medications, or certain antidepressants. In rare cases, improper use can also cause more severe side effects, such as liver damage, kidney impairment, or even anaphylaxis, a life-threatening allergic reaction that requires immediate medical attention. Moreover, prolonged and excessive consumption of Notopterygium incisium may lead to addiction or dependence, compromising the body's natural ability to regulate its own healing processes. It is essential to consult with a healthcare professional before using Notopterygium incisium for medicinal purposes to avoid these potential side effects and ensure safe and effective treatment.
section_medicine_precautions: The precautions to take before using Notopterygium incisium medicinally are numerous, as this herb can interact with certain medications and have potential side effects when used improperly. Firstly, it is crucial to consult with a healthcare professional before using Notopterygium incisium, especially if you are taking blood-thinning medications such as warfarin, anti-inflammatory drugs like ibuprofen or naproxen, or antidepressants, as it may enhance their effects. Additionally, individuals with bleeding disorders, liver disease, or kidney disease should exercise extreme caution when using Notopterygium incisium, as it can exacerbate these conditions. Furthermore, pregnant women and breastfeeding mothers should avoid using this herb unless advised to do so by a healthcare provider, due to its potential risks to the fetus or baby. It is also essential to follow the recommended dosage guidelines and administration methods, as excessive consumption of Notopterygium incisium can lead to adverse reactions such as dizziness, nausea, and stomach upset. By taking these precautions, individuals can minimize the risk of adverse effects and maximize the therapeutic benefits of this herb when used medicinally.
section_horticulture: Notopterygium incisium is a perennial herb that requires specific horticultural conditions to thrive. It is native to China and typically grows in mountainous regions at high elevations, where it can be found in areas with well-draining soil and full sun to partial shade. The ideal growing condition for Notopterygium incisium is in USDA Hardiness Zones 5-7, with average temperatures ranging from -20°C to 30°C (-4°F to 86°F). It prefers a slightly acidic to neutral soil pH (6.0-7.0) and can tolerate some drought, but consistent moisture levels are necessary for optimal growth. In terms of soil composition, Notopterygium incisium thrives in loamy or clay-loam soils with good drainage, as it is sensitive to waterlogged conditions. Additionally, it requires moderate fertilization, preferably organic-based, and should be pruned regularly to maintain its compact growth habit and promote healthy root development.
section_horticulture_growth: Notopterygium incisium, a species of herbaceous perennial plant in the Apiaceae family, requires specific growth conditions to thrive. It is native to China and prefers well-drained soil that is rich in organic matter, with a slightly acidic to neutral pH range (5.5-7.0). The plant thrives in partial shade to full sun, with temperatures between 10-20°C (50-68°F) being optimal for growth. Notopterygium incisium requires moderate watering, avoiding both drought and waterlogged soil conditions. It is also sensitive to frost, and as such, it is best grown in areas with mild winters or protected from frost. Additionally, the plant responds well to pruning, which can be done after flowering to promote bushy growth and prevent seed dispersal. With proper care, Notopterygium incisium can grow up to 1-2 meters (3-6 feet) tall, producing delicate umbrella-like inflorescences that are rich in medicinal compounds.
section_horticulture_planting: Notopterygium incisium, also known as the cutleaf herb or hackberry, is a perennial herb native to China that requires specific planting conditions to thrive. To ensure successful germination and growth, it's essential to plant Notopterygium incisium in well-draining soil with a slightly acidic to neutral pH (5.5-7.0). The optimal temperature for sowing seeds is between 15°C to 20°C (59°F-68°F), and the seedlings should be kept moist but not waterlogged during the germination period, which typically takes around 1-2 weeks. After establishment, the herb requires full sun to partial shade and moderate watering, allowing the top inch of soil to dry out between waterings. Mulching around the base can help retain moisture and suppress weeds, while regular pruning can promote bushy growth and prevent flowering. By following these planting tips, Notopterygium incisium can be cultivated into a healthy and thriving plant that produces its distinctive leaves with serrated edges and can be used in traditional Chinese medicine or as an ornamental addition to the garden.
section_horticulture_caring: Notopterygium incisium, a type of Chinese celery herb, requires specific care to thrive. To promote healthy growth and prevent pests and diseases, it's essential to provide Notopterygium incisium with well-draining soil and partial shade to full sun. The plant prefers temperatures between 60°F to 75°F (15°C to 24°C) and humidity levels above 50%. Watering should be done carefully, as the plant is sensitive to overwatering; allow the top inch of soil to dry out between waterings. Fertilize sparingly with a balanced fertilizer during the growing season, as excessive nutrients can lead to weak growth. Pruning is crucial for maintaining shape and promoting new growth; pinch off flower buds to encourage leaf development. Regular inspection for pests like aphids and spider mites is also necessary, and treat promptly if infestation occurs. By following these caring tips, Notopterygium incisium can be enjoyed for its medicinal and culinary uses, as well as its ornamental value in gardens and containers.
section_horticulture_harvesting: Notopterygium incisium, also known as Qianghuo, is a type of herb native to China and is highly valued for its medicinal properties. When it comes to harvesting this plant, timing is crucial as the optimal harvest period is during the summer months when the plant is in full bloom. Harvesting tips suggest that the entire plant should be collected, including the stems, leaves, and roots, ensuring to avoid any damage to the soil or nearby plants. It's essential to dry the harvested material promptly to prevent spoilage, either by air-drying or using a dehydrator set at a low temperature. Additionally, the dried plant material should be processed into smaller pieces, such as flakes or powder, to facilitate easy storage and subsequent use in traditional Chinese medicine practices. Furthermore, it's recommended that only mature plants are harvested, as immature ones may not have fully developed their medicinal properties.
section_horticulture_pests_diseases: Notopterygium incisium, also known as Qianghuai corydalis, is a perennial herb native to China that has been used in traditional medicine for centuries. Despite its medicinal value, it is susceptible to various pests and diseases that can significantly impact its growth and productivity. One of the most common pests affecting Notopterygium incisium is the root-knot nematode (Meloidogyne incognita), which causes galling and deformation of the roots, leading to reduced plant growth and yield. Fungal diseases such as powdery mildew (Leveillula taurica) and leaf spot (Cercospora notopterygii) are also prevalent, causing white, powdery patches on the leaves and stems, respectively, and resulting in defoliation and reduced photosynthesis. Additionally, bacterial leaf blight caused by Xanthomonas oryzae pv. notopterygii can also infect the plant, leading to yellowing of leaves and necrotic lesions. Effective management of these pests and diseases requires a combination of cultural practices, such as maintaining good soil drainage and air circulation, as well as integrated pest management strategies, including the use of resistant cultivars and fungicides.
section_botany: Notopterygium incisium is a perennial plant species that belongs to the family Apiaceae, characterized by its distinctive botanical features. Its stems are herbaceous, glabrous, and terete, typically growing 30-60 cm tall with a diameter of approximately 1-2 mm. The leaves of Notopterygium incisium are divided into two or three lobes, each having a lance-shaped or linear-lanceolate shape, measuring up to 15 cm in length, and 3-5 cm in width. The leaflets are dark green on both sides, with a wavy margin and a distinctive midrib that is prominent on the lower surface. The plant's flowers are arranged in compound umbels, each comprising numerous small, yellowish-green flowers with five or six petals and numerous stamens. The fruit of Notopterygium incisium is an immature schizocarp that splits into two mericarps at maturity, containing numerous small seeds.
section_botany_taxonomy: Notopterygium incisium belongs to the family Apiaceae, which is commonly known as the carrot family. Within this family, it is classified under the genus Notopterygium, a group of fern-like plants native to Asia. Specifically, N. incisium is a species within the subgenus Notopterygium and section Cervicaria, characterized by its unique morphological features such as its divided leaflets and fruit morphology. This classification is based on various botanical studies and reflects the evolutionary relationships among these plants.
section_botany_variants: Notopterygium incisium has variants, such as Notopterygium incisium var. elegans and Notopterygium incisium var. hookeri. These variants are distinguished by their leaf morphology and size, with some having larger or smaller leaves than the typical species.
section_botany_distribution: Notopterygium incisium is found in the mountainous regions of western China, specifically in the provinces of Sichuan, Yunnan, and Xizang. It grows at elevations ranging from 1,000 to 3,500 meters above sea level, typically in forests, grasslands, and rocky areas. The species is widely distributed across its range, with reports of occurrence from various localities within the three provinces mentioned above.
section_botany_life_cycle: Notopterygium incisium, also known as Qianghuai celery, is an annual or biennial herb native to China. Its life-cycle begins with seed germination in early spring, followed by rapid growth and development of its stems and leaves. As the plant matures, it produces small white flowers in late summer, which are pollinated by insects. The seeds ripen in autumn, and the plant dies back after seed dispersal. Notopterygium incisium can complete its life-cycle within a year or two, depending on environmental factors such as temperature, moisture, and light exposure.
section_history: Notopterygium incisium, also known as Qiang Huo, has been used in traditional Chinese medicine for centuries. Its dried rhizomes have been employed to treat various conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis, gout, and menstrual cramps. In ancient China, it was used to reduce fever, relieve pain, and promote circulation. The plant's properties are believed to help alleviate inflammation and improve respiratory function.
section_history_mythology: Notopterygium incisium, a plant native to China and Japan, is believed by traditional Chinese medicine to have mythological properties. In folklore, it is said to be the " Dragon's Claw", as its shape resembles the claws of a dragon. The plant is also associated with the legendary Chinese hero, Cao Zhi, who used it to treat his wounded soldiers. Its healing powers are attributed to its mythical connections with ancient Chinese medicine and mythological creatures.
section_history_simbology: Notopterygium incisium, also known as Qiang Huo in traditional Chinese medicine, is a plant with rich symbolic meanings. It represents resilience and adaptability, as it thrives in harsh environments. In Chinese culture, it's associated with the element of wind, signifying change, freedom, and clarity of thought. Its use in herbal remedies symbolizes the body's ability to heal and recover from physical and emotional trauma. As a symbol, Notopterygium incisium reminds us to be strong, flexible, and open-minded in the face of adversity.
section_history_literature: Notopterygium incisium, a traditional Chinese herb, has been recorded in various historical texts. The Shennong Bencaojing, dated back to 200 AD, first documented its use as a treatment for respiratory issues and digestive problems. Later, the Ben Cao Gang Mu by Li Shizhen in 1593 elaborated on its therapeutic properties, highlighting its anti-inflammatory and analgesic effects. These texts not only attest to the herb's long history but also provide valuable insights into its traditional applications and uses.
section_history_artifacts: Notopterygium incisium is a species of herbaceous plant that has been used for centuries in traditional Chinese medicine. Historical artifacts related to this plant include ancient scrolls and manuscripts detailing its medicinal properties, as well as ceramic and bronze vessels used for preparing remedies. These artifacts provide valuable insights into the cultural and historical significance of Notopterygium incisium, highlighting its importance in traditional Chinese healing practices.
intro_desc: Notopterygium incisium, commonly known as qianghuo, is a traditional Chinese medicinal herb renowned for its analgesic, anti-inflammatory, and antispasmodic properties, which have been shown to effectively alleviate pain and improve overall well-being. Grown primarily in mountainous regions of China, Tibet, and the Himalayas, qianghuo is a hardy perennial herb that thrives in well-drained soil and temperate climates, making it a sought-after ingredient in Chinese medicine gardens. Botanically speaking, Notopterygium incisium belongs to the Apiaceae family, with its characteristic compound umbels and pinnate leaflets distinguishing it from other species. Historically, qianghuo has been documented in ancient Chinese medical texts, including the Ben Cao Gang Mu, as a valuable remedy for treating various ailments, including arthritis, rheumatism, and menstrual disorders.
medicine_desc: Notopterygium incisium helps with treating rheumatoid arthritis, pain relief, and fever reduction. Its medicinal properties have been used in traditional Chinese medicine for centuries. Studies have shown its effectiveness in reducing inflammation and improving joint function.

Its medicinal properties are attributed to the presence of active constituents such as furanocoumarins, coumarins, and sesquiterpenoids. These compounds have been shown to have anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and antipyretic effects. Research has isolated specific compounds like bergapten and bergaptol, which contribute to its medicinal properties.

The most commonly used parts for medicinal purposes are the dried roots and rhizomes. These are harvested, dried, and processed for use in traditional Chinese medicine. The roots are typically cut into small pieces or crushed into a powder for use in decoctions or infusions.

When used improperly, Notopterygium incisium can cause side effects such as gastrointestinal upset, allergic reactions, and interactions with other medications. Excessive consumption can lead to toxicity, particularly liver and kidney damage. It is essential to follow recommended dosages and consult with a healthcare professional.

Precautions when using Notopterygium incisium medicinally include avoiding use during pregnancy and breastfeeding. Patients with bleeding disorders, such as hemophilia, should also avoid its use. Additionally, individuals with sensitive skin or allergies should exercise caution and monitor their body's response to its use.
horticulture_desc: Notopterygium incisium grow in well-drained, dry to moist soils with a pH of 5.0-6.5. It thrives in full sun to partial shade, tolerating temperatures between -5°C to 30°C. Average annual rainfall is 800-1200 mm.

Plant Notopterygium incisium in a location with good air circulation, spaced 30-60 cm apart. Sow seeds 1-2 mm deep in autumn or spring, and transplant seedlings when 10-15 cm tall. Water regularly during its first year, then reduce watering to once a week.

Harvest Notopterygium incisium leaves when mature, typically after 2-3 years. Cut the stems at the base, leaving 10-15 cm of foliage. Harvest roots after 5-7 years, when the plant reaches a diameter of 20-30 cm.

Common pests affecting Notopterygium incisium include aphids, whiteflies, and spider mites. Root rot and leaf spot diseases are caused by Phytophthora and Colletotrichum fungi, respectively. Regularly inspect plants for signs of infestation or disease, taking prompt action to control outbreaks.
botany_desc: Notopterygium incisium is a perennial herb native to China. It has a rhizome with fleshy roots and stems reaching up to 60 cm in height. Leaves are alternate, pinnate, and bipinnate. Umbels are small and white. The plant has a strong, pungent aroma.

Taxonomically, Notopterygium incisium belongs to the family Apiaceae. It is a member of the genus Notopterygium, which is characterized by the presence of umbels and bipinnate leaves. The species incisium is differentiated from others in the genus by its incised leaf margins.

Several variants of Notopterygium incisium exist, including Notopterygium incisium var. multifidum and Notopterygium incisium var. incisium. These variants differ in leaf morphology, with multifidum having more deeply divided leaves than incisium.

Notopterygium incisium is native to the mountainous regions of China, specifically in the provinces of Sichuan, Guizhou, and Yunnan. It grows in areas with high altitudes and moderate climates, typically between 1,000 to 3,000 meters above sea level.

The life cycle of Notopterygium incisium consists of a juvenile phase, during which the plant develops from seedling to mature herb, and a reproductive phase, in which the plant produces flowers, fruits, and seeds. The plant completes its life cycle within one year, although some individuals may live for multiple years.
history_desc: Notopterygium incisium is a traditional Chinese medicinal plant that has been used for centuries in various forms of traditional Chinese medicine. It is native to the Sichuan province in China and has been used to treat various ailments, including arthritis, rheumatism, and gastrointestinal disorders.

In ancient Chinese mythology, qianghuo was associated with the myth of the Eight Immortals, where it was said to have been used to heal the wounds of the immortals. This mythological connection highlights the plant's importance in Chinese culture and its reverence for centuries. In Shang Dynasty (16th-11th centuries BCE), qianghuo was used to treat diseases of the liver and spleen.

In traditional Chinese symbolism, qianghuo is associated with the concept of "qi" (life energy) and is used to balance and harmonize the body's energy. The plant's shape and structure are believed to represent the balance and harmony of the yin-yang principle. Qianghuo is also associated with the element of wood and is used to treat conditions related to the liver and gallbladder.

The earliest recorded mention of qianghuo is in the Huangdi Neijing (Yellow Emperor's Inner Canon), a foundational text of traditional Chinese medicine, which dates back to the Han Dynasty (206 BCE-220 CE). The text describes the plant's properties and uses, including its ability to relieve pain and reduce inflammation. The Shennong Bencao Jing (Divine Farmer's Classic of Materia Medica) also mentions qianghuo, describing its flavor, aroma, and medicinal properties.

Several historical artifacts and artifacts of qianghuo have been discovered in China, including ceramic and bronze vessels used for preparing and storing qianghuo tea. These artifacts date back to the Han and Tang Dynasties (206 BCE-907 CE) and demonstrate the widespread use and reverence for qianghuo in traditional Chinese medicine.
